title: Role of quantum confinement in semiconductor-superconductor core-shell nanowires
authors: Tudor Gabriel Dumitru, Anna Sitek, Gunnar Thorgilsson, Sigurdur I. Erlingsson, Tudor Dan Stanescu, Andrei Manolescu
abstract: This work is motivated by the experimentally observed coherence of the supercurrent in semiconductor nanowires covered by a half-shell metallic superconductor, which leads to flux dependent supercurrent oscillations with period h/2e, as expected for a tubular superconductor, i.e. Little-Parks oscillations. We perform microscopic model calculations and compare the results for full and half metallic shells. We use an effective Hamiltonian derived from the Green's function of the proximitized semiconductor nanowire, where the presence of the superconductor is represented by a self energy. Furthermore, we incorporate the electrostatic band-bending at the metal-semiconductor interface as a rectangular narrow quantum well on the semiconductor side. The properties of the eigenstates of the effective Hamiltonian are determined by the spatial profile of the corresponding transverse modes in the normal state. For half-shell wires, transverse modes with high-enough energy expand outside the interface quantum well and generate eigenstates with mixed electron-hole character that surround the entire circumference of the nanowire, similar to eigenstates of the full-shell system. We identify these states as being responsible for the observed Little-Parks effect.
summary: This theoretical study models the induced superconductivity in semiconductor nanowires coated by a superconductor shell. By comparing full and half-shell geometries, the authors show that while both can exhibit superconducting behavior, the gap size is strongly dependent on structural details like partial coverage. The work provides critical insights into how quantum confinement dictates the stability of tubular supercurrents.
main_problem: The work investigates how quantum confinement and superconducting coupling affect induced superconductivity in semiconductor-superconductor core-shell nanowires.
main_result: Both full-shell and half-shell geometries can support tubular superconducting states, but the induced gap size is highly sensitive to the degree of superconducting coverage (half vs. full) and the chemical potential ($\mu$).
method: The study employs microscopic model calculations using an effective Bogoliubov-de Gennes (BdG) Hamiltonian derived from Green's functions.
model_or_system: The system is a core-shell nanowire heterostructure, where the semiconductor core is coupled to a metallic superconductor shell (either full or half coverage). The model incorporates electrostatic band-bending via a rectangular quantum well potential and superconducting proximity effects via an interface self-energy.
key_observables: Induced superconducting gap ($\Delta_{	ext{ind}}$), electron/hole character of eigenstates, supercurrent oscillations exhibiting Little-Parks periodicity.
important_parameters: Superconducting gap ($\Delta$), coupling strength ($\gamma$), chemical potential ($\mu$), and the geometry (full vs. half shell).
main_assumptions: The complex electrostatic potential is approximated by a thin rectangular quantum well, and the superconducting contribution is modeled via an interface self-energy term in the low-energy limit.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the cross-section of the nanowires (full/half shell), the Density of States ($ho(E)$) showing gap formation, energy spectra from the BdG Hamiltonian for different geometries and $\mu$, and spatial profiles of electron/hole components.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by establishing the physical model using an effective Hamiltonian, calculating transverse modes, determining the superconducting properties via the self-energy, and finally analyzing how geometry (full vs. half shell) and external parameters ($\mu$, barrier height) modulate the induced gap and tubular superconductivity.
why_interesting: The detailed analysis of proximity effects in low-dimensional hybrid systems using BdG theory is highly relevant to understanding superconducting quantum circuits and topological materials, bridging concepts from condensed matter theory with potential device applications.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Cross section of the hybrid core-shell nanowires with (a) full-shell and (b) half-shell superconductor layers. The white region in the center is the (insulating) semiconductor core, while the blue and green regions represent the (conduct- ing) semiconductor shell, where charge carriers are present. The green zone indicates the QW potential created at the metal-semiconductor contact. The red line corresponds to the self-energy which in our model represents the interface with the superconducting layer.

FIG. 2. (a1-a6) Wave functions corresponding to the lowest energy transverse modes of a full shell nanowire, in increas- ing energy. The green shading indicates the QW potential. Modes (a2), (a3), and (a6) are double degenerated because of the hexagonal symmetry. Note that the electrons are shifted towards the external boundaries, where the QW potential is located. (b1-b6) Transverse wave functions for the half shell case, with metal-semiconductor contact on three facets. Note that the lowest four energy modes (b1-b4) are localized near the metal-semiconductor interface, while the 5-th and 6-th modes extend throughout the entire semiconductor shell, i.e. they are tubular-type states similar...

FIG. 3. Energy spectra for the nanowire with infinite length in the normal state. The transverse energies correspond to the bottom of each parabola, relatively to the ground state which is set at zero energy. (a) Full shell case, where the degeneracy sequence is 2, 4, 4, 2 due to orbital and spin degeneracies [48]. (b) Half shell case, where the orbital degeneracy is lifted, and all parabolas are only 2-fold spin degenerate.

FIG. 4. Density of states for (a) the full shell model and (b) the half shell model with several sets of parameters. The values of the chemical potential are explicitly given in panel (a) in meV units. Full lines correspond to a superconductor- semiconductor coupling constant γ = 83 meV and a QW po- tential of −50 meV. The dotted lines represent the DOS of hybrid systems with reduced semiconductor-superconductor coupling, γ = 41.5 meV, while the dashed lines correspond to a vanishing QW potential.

FIG. 5. Low energy BdG spectra of infinite length nanowires within the static approximation. The full shell case is shown in panels (a1) and (a2) with µ = 3.3 meV and µ = 6.6 meV, respectively. The half shell case is shown in panels (b1) and (b2), again with µ = 3.3 and µ = 6.6 meV. The green hor- izontal lines indicate the superconducting gap of the parent superconductor, ∆= 0.33 meV. Note that the static approxi- mation is highly accurate for energies E ≲0.4∆≈0.13 meV, i.e. near the gap edge.
